title: Three-point lighting + motivated light (film lighting 101 → set practice)
sources_note: Distilled from StudioBinder three-point guides, film-school Lighting 101, and set practice: practicals motivate key/fill/edge.
core_principle: Three-point is a teaching scaffold; on set, lights should feel motivated by the world of the scene.

key.job: Primary direction and modeling on face/subject; strongest readable source
key.placement_hint: Typically 30–45° off camera axis, slightly above eye line

fill.job: Controls shadow depth from key; softer and weaker than key
fill.ratio_notes: Lower fill → higher drama/contrast; higher fill → openness/safety

back_or_edge.job: Separates subject from background; optional hair/edge rim

background.job: Shape space depth without competing with face

suspicion.notes: Keep eyes; let shadows hide truth

oppression.notes: Harder key; protect eyes unless hiding

revelation.notes: Truth lands on face; object inserts sharp

dread.notes: Pools of dark; motivated cold edge

hard_rules: Prefer motivated sources that could exist in the story world
Protect eyes in low key unless story deliberately hides them
Do not invent cinematic lighting jargon without a visible source description for prompts
INSERT/object: light the material (paper grain, metal edge), not a portrait setup

prefer: soft window key from camera left
warm practical lamp as key, cool ambient fill
low-key pool of light on desk, deep shadows
